questions.question: What are the stages of compilation in order?
questions.correctAnswer: Preprocessor, Compiler (front-end, optimizer, back-end), Assembler, Linker
questions.explanation: .cpp → preprocessor (.i) → compiler: front-end (AST), optimizer (IR), back-end (assembly) → assembler (.o) → linker (executable).

questions.question: What does the compiler front-end do?
options: Lexing, parsing, semantic analysis — produces AST and IR from C++ source
questions.correctAnswer: Lexing, parsing, semantic analysis — produces AST and IR from C++ source
questions.explanation: Front-end: lexer → tokens, parser → AST, semantic analysis → typed AST, IR generation. This is where C++ syntax errors are caught.

questions.question: What does the optimizer do?
options: Transforms IR to faster equivalent: inlining, dead code elimination, loop unrolling, constant folding, vectorization
questions.correctAnswer: Transforms IR to faster equivalent: inlining, dead code elimination, loop unrolling, constant folding, vectorization
questions.explanation: Optimizer works on IR (GIMPLE/LLVM IR). Most passes are target-independent. -O0 skips most; -O2 is standard; -O3 is aggressive.

questions.question: What does the linker do?
options: Resolves symbols: connects each 'require' (undefined symbol) to a 'provide' (definition) across .o files and libraries
questions.correctAnswer: Resolves symbols: connects each 'require' (undefined symbol) to a 'provide' (definition) across .o files and libraries
questions.explanation: Each .o has symbol table: exports (defines) and imports (references). Linker matches them. Static: copies code. Dynamic: PLT/GOT stubs.

questions.question: What is an object file (.o)?
options: Binary machine code + symbol table + relocation entries — not yet executable
questions.correctAnswer: Binary machine code + symbol table + relocation entries — not yet executable
questions.explanation: .o contains compiled code but unresolved symbols. Linker combines .o files, resolves relocations, produces executable.

questions.question: What is the difference between static and dynamic linking?
options: Static: library code copied into executable at build time. Dynamic: library loaded at runtime, shared across processes
questions.correctAnswer: Static: library code copied into executable at build time. Dynamic: library loaded at runtime, shared across processes
questions.explanation: Static: larger binary, no runtime dependency. Dynamic: smaller binary, shared .so/.dll, can update library without recompiling.

questions.question: What are PLT and GOT in dynamic linking?
options: Procedure Linkage Table and Global Offset Table — lazy binding: first call resolves address via dynamic linker, subsequent calls jump directly
questions.correctAnswer: Procedure Linkage Table and Global Offset Table — lazy binding: first call resolves address via dynamic linker, subsequent calls jump directly
questions.explanation: PLT stub → GOT entry. First call: GOT points to dynamic linker (resolve). After: GOT updated to actual function address. Lazy binding.

questions.question: What is ASLR?
options: Address Space Layout Randomization — OS randomizes base addresses of stack, heap, libraries each execution to prevent exploits
questions.correctAnswer: Address Space Layout Randomization — OS randomizes base addresses of stack, heap, libraries each execution to prevent exploits
questions.explanation: ASLR = security through unpredictability. Attacker can't hardcode addresses. PIE (Position Independent Executable) required.

questions.question: What is an ELF file?
options: Executable and Linkable Format — the standard binary format on Linux (like PE on Windows, Mach-O on macOS)
questions.correctAnswer: Executable and Linkable Format — the standard binary format on Linux (like PE on Windows, Mach-O on macOS)
questions.explanation: ELF contains: header, program headers (segments for loader), section headers (.text, .data, .bss, .rodata), symbol tables.

questions.question: What is relocation?
options: The linker adjusting addresses in machine code to reflect the final layout in memory — fixing up references between .o files
questions.correctAnswer: The linker adjusting addresses in machine code to reflect the final layout in memory — fixing up references between .o files
questions.explanation: .o files have relocation entries: 'this address needs to point to symbol X'. Linker patches these with final addresses.

questions.question: What is a syscall?
options: A controlled transition from user mode (ring 3) to kernel mode (ring 0) — the only way user code accesses hardware/OS services
questions.correctAnswer: A controlled transition from user mode (ring 3) to kernel mode (ring 0) — the only way user code accesses hardware/OS services
questions.explanation: Syscall = user→kernel transition. Examples: read(), write(), open(), mmap(). syscall instruction on x86-64. Cost: ~100-300 cycles.

questions.question: What are CPU protection rings?
options: Privilege levels: ring 0 (kernel, full access), ring 3 (user, restricted). Syscalls transition 3→0.
questions.correctAnswer: Privilege levels: ring 0 (kernel, full access), ring 3 (user, restricted). Syscalls transition 3→0.
questions.explanation: x86 has 4 rings but only 0 and 3 are used. Ring 0: execute privileged instructions, access all memory. Ring 3: restricted.

questions.question: How does the SYSCALL instruction work?
options: Atomically: saves RIP to RCX, loads RIP from LSTAR MSR (kernel handler), switches to ring 0. SYSRET reverses.
questions.correctAnswer: Atomically: saves RIP to RCX, loads RIP from LSTAR MSR (kernel handler), switches to ring 0. SYSRET reverses.
questions.explanation: SYSCALL: RCX←RIP, RIP←LSTAR, R11←RFLAGS, RFLAGS←RFLAGS & ~FMASK. No stack switch (uses MSRs). ~100-300 cycles.

questions.question: Why are syscalls expensive?
options: Mode switch (ring 3→0), register save/restore, kernel validation of arguments, potentially TLB flush, Spectre mitigations
questions.correctAnswer: Mode switch (ring 3→0), register save/restore, kernel validation of arguments, potentially TLB flush, Spectre mitigations
questions.explanation: ~100-300 cycles minimum. More with KPTI (Meltdown mitigation: page tables switch). VDSO maps some syscalls to userspace for speed.

questions.question: What is io_uring (Linux)?
options: A high-performance async I/O interface using shared ring buffers — submit and complete queues mapped in userspace, minimal syscalls
questions.correctAnswer: A high-performance async I/O interface using shared ring buffers — submit and complete queues mapped in userspace, minimal syscalls
questions.explanation: io_uring: submission queue (SQ) and completion queue (CQ) shared between kernel and userspace. Batch I/O ops with minimal syscall overhead.

questions.question: What does the call instruction do?
options: Pushes return address (RIP_next) onto stack and jumps to function address
questions.correctAnswer: Pushes return address (RIP_next) onto stack and jumps to function address
questions.explanation: call = push return address + jmp. ret = pop RIP. Pure hardware — no OS, no heap, no allocation.

questions.question: What register points to the top of the stack?
questions.correctAnswer: RSP (Stack Pointer)
questions.explanation: RSP points to the current stack top (lowest active address). push decrements RSP; pop increments it. Stack grows downward.

questions.question: What is a stack frame?
options: The region of stack used by one function call — contains return address, saved RBP, local variables
questions.correctAnswer: The region of stack used by one function call — contains return address, saved RBP, local variables
questions.explanation: Prologue creates frame (push rbp; mov rbp,rsp; sub rsp,N). Epilogue destroys it (mov rsp,rbp; pop rbp; ret). RBP = fixed anchor.

questions.question: What is the System V AMD64 calling convention?
options: Linux/macOS ABI: first 6 integer args in RDI, RSI, RDX, RCX, R8, R9. Return in RAX. Red zone below RSP.
questions.correctAnswer: Linux/macOS ABI: first 6 integer args in RDI, RSI, RDX, RCX, R8, R9. Return in RAX. Red zone below RSP.
questions.explanation: System V: 6 register args, red zone (128 bytes below RSP usable without adjustment). Microsoft x64: 4 register args, shadow space.

questions.question: What three registers matter most for function calls?
questions.correctAnswer: RIP (instruction pointer — next instruction), RSP (stack pointer — top of stack), RBP (base pointer — frame anchor)
acceptableAnswers: instruction pointer, stack pointer, base pointer
questions.explanation: RIP: what to execute next. RSP: current stack top. RBP: fixed reference within frame. RSP moves; RBP stays fixed.

questions.question: What does the function prologue do?
options: push rbp; mov rbp,rsp; sub rsp,N — saves caller's frame pointer, establishes new base, allocates locals
questions.correctAnswer: push rbp; mov rbp,rsp; sub rsp,N — saves caller's frame pointer, establishes new base, allocates locals
questions.explanation: Prologue = enter function. push rbp saves old frame. mov rbp,rsp sets new frame. sub rsp,N allocates N bytes for locals.

questions.question: What does the function epilogue do?
options: mov rsp,rbp; pop rbp; ret — deallocates locals, restores caller's frame, pops return address
questions.correctAnswer: mov rsp,rbp; pop rbp; ret — deallocates locals, restores caller's frame, pops return address
questions.explanation: Epilogue = leave function. reverse of prologue. leave = mov rsp,rbp + pop rbp (one byte vs three).

questions.question: Why is stack allocation so fast?
options: Single sub instruction on RSP — no syscall, no malloc, no GC. Deallocation = add. One cycle.
questions.correctAnswer: Single sub instruction on RSP — no syscall, no malloc, no GC. Deallocation = add. One cycle.
questions.explanation: sub rsp, 4096 = allocate 4KB in one instruction. add rsp, 4096 = free. Compare: malloc(4096) = potentially syscall + page table updates.

questions.question: Why must RSP be 16-byte aligned at call?
options: SSE movaps requires 16-byte aligned memory — faults on misaligned addresses. ABI mandates alignment.
questions.correctAnswer: SSE movaps requires 16-byte aligned memory — faults on misaligned addresses. ABI mandates alignment.
questions.explanation: call pushes 8 bytes (return address), misaligning to 8 mod 16. push rbp adds 8, realigning to 16. Locals rounded to 16-byte multiples.

questions.question: What is the red zone (System V only)?
options: 128 bytes BELOW RSP that the current function can use without adjusting RSP — signal handlers skip it
questions.correctAnswer: 128 bytes BELOW RSP that the current function can use without adjusting RSP — signal handlers skip it
questions.explanation: Red zone = System V only. Windows has NO red zone — below RSP is volatile (interrupts may overwrite). Cross-platform code: never assume it.

questions.question: What is the shadow space (Microsoft x64)?
options: Caller MUST allocate 32 bytes on stack before call — callee may spill its 4 register args there
questions.correctAnswer: Caller MUST allocate 32 bytes on stack before call — callee may spill its 4 register args there
questions.explanation: Shadow space = 32 bytes reserved by caller. Simplifies variadic functions and debugging. System V has no shadow space.

questions.question: How does System V differ from MS x64 for argument registers?
options: SysV: RDI,RSI,RDX,RCX,R8,R9 (6 regs). MS: RCX,RDX,R8,R9 (4 regs). SysV has red zone; MS has shadow space.
questions.correctAnswer: SysV: RDI,RSI,RDX,RCX,R8,R9 (6 regs). MS: RCX,RDX,R8,R9 (4 regs). SysV has red zone; MS has shadow space.
questions.explanation: Key differences: 6 vs 4 arg registers, RDI/RSI callee-saved on Windows (caller-saved on SysV — #1 porting trap), red zone vs shadow space.

questions.question: What is a stack buffer overflow?
options: Writing past the end of a stack buffer overwrites saved RBP and return address — attacker controls RIP on ret
questions.correctAnswer: Writing past the end of a stack buffer overwrites saved RBP and return address — attacker controls RIP on ret
questions.explanation: Buffer at [RBP-N], saved RBP at [RBP], return address at [RBP+8]. Overflow upward overwrites return address. ret → attacker code.

questions.question: What is a stack canary?
options: A random value placed between locals and saved RBP — checked before ret. If corrupted (overflow) → abort.
questions.correctAnswer: A random value placed between locals and saved RBP — checked before ret. If corrupted (overflow) → abort.
questions.explanation: Canary = compiler-inserted guard value. Overwrite buffer → corrupt canary → detected at epilogue check. Bypassable if canary leaked.

questions.question: What is ROP (Return-Oriented Programming)?
options: Chaining existing code snippets (gadgets ending in ret) to execute arbitrary logic — bypasses NX/DEP
questions.correctAnswer: Chaining existing code snippets (gadgets ending in ret) to execute arbitrary logic — bypasses NX/DEP
questions.explanation: NX bit makes stack non-executable. ROP reuses existing .text code instead of injecting new code. Gadget = short instruction sequence + ret.

questions.question: What protects against ROP at the hardware level?
options: Intel CET Shadow Stack — CPU maintains hardware-protected copy of return addresses; ret compares against it
questions.correctAnswer: Intel CET Shadow Stack — CPU maintains hardware-protected copy of return addresses; ret compares against it
questions.explanation: Shadow stack = hardware-enforced. Every call pushes to shadow stack; ret pops and compares. Mismatch → #CP fault. Hard to bypass.
